title: The Right Way to Deal With AI Data Centers
dek: A slower news cycle gives NLW room to defuse one of AI's most contentious fights — data centers — by actually checking the water and electricity numbers. Plus a Mythos/NSA correction, OpenAI's cyber escalation, another SpaceX compute deal, and a $200B Google wobble over two departing researchers.

oneIdea.headline: The data center fight needs a middle path between rollover and pitchforks.
oneIdea.summary: Both sides of the data center debate are wildly reductive. The scary water and electricity numbers mostly don't survive contact with context — Amazon's entire global data center fleet uses a day's worth of US golf course watering. But communities aren't powerless either. The real opportunity isn't 'let big tech do whatever' or 'stop it entirely' — it's negotiating for serious economic benefits, like the Louisiana parish where teachers got $50,000 bonuses funded by Meta's campus. Move past the binary into the how-everyone-wins phase.

gpCorner.note: AI commentary for UK general practice; not from the podcast

items.title: How big numbers mislead - a lesson that travels to health statistics
items.body: The edition's sharpest point is that astronomical-sounding figures grab attention precisely because most people have no sense of scale: Amazon's entire global data-centre water use for a year is roughly a single day of watering America's golf courses, and US leaky pipes waste about fifteen times all data-centre water combined. The same trick runs through health reporting - a 'doubling of risk' that moves a tiny absolute number, or a screening benefit quoted relatively rather than as numbers-needed-to-treat. If NLW is right, the antidote is always context and a denominator; if Zitron is right that headline numbers are often manufactured, the discipline is the same. It is a useful reminder for explaining risk to patients, and for reading the next alarming AI-and-NHS headline.

items.title: The 'how-everyone-wins' framing applied to AI in the surgery
items.body: NLW argues communities should stop choosing between rolling over and grabbing pitchforks, and instead negotiate concrete benefits - he cites a Louisiana parish where data-centre tax receipts funded fifty-thousand-dollar teacher bonuses. The parallel for general practice is to refuse the binary of either swallowing whatever a vendor offers or rejecting AI outright. When an integrated care board or supplier brings an AI scribe or triage tool, the practical questions are what the practice gets in return: training, data control, fair pricing, and a share of any efficiency, not just a licence fee.

items.title: But check whether the benefit is real before you bargain for it
items.body: Zitron's contrasting caution is worth holding alongside: he argues much of the claimed build-out is smaller and slower than the headlines suggest. The clinical translation is to verify that an AI tool's promised benefit is actually delivered and measured before reorganising around it. If NLW is right, there is genuine value to negotiate over; if Zitron is right, some of it is vapour - so insist on demonstrated, audited time-savings or safety gains in your own setting, not a vendor's slide.

the_other_side.source: Where Are All The Data Centers?
the_other_side.text: NLW wants communities to move past the binary and bargain hard with a real, valuable build-out. Zitron's prior question is whether that build-out is as real and as large as everyone assumes. He spent a week hunting for big data centres that broke ground in 2023 or 2024 and were actually finished and switched on, and came up, in his words, "surprisingly empty-handed." He argues nobody has yet built a true one-gigawatt facility, that "operational" routinely describes a campus where only a handful of the planned buildings have any gear inside, and that hyperscalers refuse to disclose how much capacity is genuinely online despite over eight hundred billion dollars of capex. On his telling, the fight over a data centre's water and electricity quietly assumes a scale that the disclosures do not actually support.

the_truth_beneath.text: Both men orbit one variable: how real and how large the AI data centre build-out actually is. Both accept that data centres exist, are being built and draw real water and power, and that reporting is muddy. The empirical question that will resolve it is how much capacity has been completed and switched on; the observable that settles it is disclosed, audited operational critical-IT load - finished megawatts and gigawatts, completion timelines, honest hyperscaler figures. The residue is pure worldview: Whittemore treats the build-out as a real, valuable thing worth negotiating local benefits over; Zitron treats much of the claimed capacity as phased public-relations vapour. Whittemore is vindicated if large finished capacity is verifiably online and communities win real concessions from real campuses; Zitron if gigawatt claims keep dissolving into a few populated buildings. Both are polemicists with incentives - Whittemore bullish and anti-doom, Zitron bubble-certain and anti-hype with a newsletter - so the signal is their shared distrust of headline numbers: he doubts the capacity figures, NLW the scary impact figures. Watch whether hyperscalers ever disclose real operational capacity.

headlines.headline: The Mythos-hacked-the-NSA story was a misread
headlines.summary: An Economist reporter issued a correction: Senator Warner misunderstood NSA Director Rudd. The 'hours not weeks' wording was real, but Mythos's use was a controlled red-team exercise testing internal networks — not a live cyberattack — and the agency's red teams no longer have access to it.

headlines.headline: Mythos doesn't let anyone hack the NSA — but it speeds up the ones who get in
headlines.summary: Per cybersecurity account Iris C2, gaining initial access to air-gapped classified systems remains the hard part. The real takeaway: once inside, Mythos makes exploit design and execution much faster, cutting the time defenders have to detect and contain an intruder.

headlines.headline: OpenAI ships GPT 5.5 Cyber, claims it beats Mythos
headlines.summary: As part of its Daybreak initiative — OpenAI's answer to Anthropic's Project Glasswing — OpenAI launched the full GPT 5.5 Cyber, its first cybersecurity-tuned model with reduced guardrails. It claims the model now overtakes Mythos on the CyberGym benchmark.

headlines.headline: Frontier defensive capabilities should not be concentrated in the hands of a few.
headlines.summary: OpenAI positioned Daybreak as more open than Glasswing, inviting smaller organizations to apply for access so defenders everywhere can find and fix vulnerabilities before attackers exploit them.

headlines.headline: Patch the Planet finds hundreds of bugs, ships 37 fixes
headlines.summary: OpenAI launched Patch the Planet with Trail of Bits to secure open-source libraries. Initial testing of GPT 5.5 Cyber surfaced hundreds of bugs; 37 patches are deployed with many more in the pipeline and 30+ projects already enrolled.

headlines.headline: The expensive part of security work has moved. The advantage is no longer in finding bugs but everything after.
headlines.summary: Trail of Bits argues the value has shifted to confirming findings, getting severity right, writing patches maintainers will accept, and coordinating disclosure — exactly the work that floods of AI-generated reports threaten to bury.

headlines.headline: Five Eyes issue a rare AI cyber-risk alert
headlines.summary: The intelligence alliance warned that frontier AI is rapidly transforming cyber risk and that assumptions can become outdated in months, not years. The UK NCSC bulletin urged businesses to integrate AI into security ops and treat cyber as 'a core business risk and leadership responsibility,' not a purely technical issue.

headlines.headline: Trump orders a push for a working quantum computer by 2028
headlines.summary: Two new executive orders direct federal agencies to partner with industry on a quantum computer for research (OSTP's Kratsios targets 2028), mandate migration to quantum-secure cryptography by 2031, and harden the quantum supply chain against adversaries.

headlines.headline: SpaceX signs a $6.3B compute deal with Reflection AI
headlines.summary: Open-source startup Reflection AI agreed to rent Colossus II capacity through 2029 for $6.3B total, with three-month walk-away terms. It's notably smaller than the Anthropic and Google SpaceX deals running near $1B a month each.

headlines.headline: Reflection pitches itself as the domestic open-source alternative
headlines.summary: Yet to ship its first frontier model, Reflection used the deal to argue that nations and enterprises increasingly recognize 'the risks and costs associated with exclusively depending on closed models.' It's working with the Pentagon and DOE's Genesis Mission.

headlines.headline: SpaceX as Neo Cloud plus Neo Lab is a 'crazy effective combo'
headlines.summary: Swigs of Layton Space argued no one is doing the math right: SpaceX has already recouped about half its Cursor investment via compute deals, with the rest covered if Composer 3 does well. No other company is simultaneously a leading lab and a Neo cloud where GPUs are concerned.

headlines.headline: Two researcher exits, $200B in lost Google market cap
headlines.summary: Google stock fell as much as 7.2% Monday — its largest intraday move since February — after Nobel laureate John Jumper left DeepMind for Anthropic and Noam Shazeer left for OpenAI. NLW thinks the market over-indexes on AI headlines, but a real narrative shift around Google's pace is underway.

headlines.headline: Google is losing the war for talent at the frontier of AI.
headlines.summary: D.A. Davidson's Gil Luria argued Google held a state-of-the-art model for only weeks last year and has fallen off since, with these departures suggesting it is falling further behind on enterprise-essential agentic use cases.

headlines.headline: NLW sticks to his guns on overreacting to personnel moves
headlines.summary: Citing Prime Intellect's Florian, NLW notes the recurring cycle: 'peak Google is done for' is always followed by Google releasing a new model and everyone deciding no one can match its TPUs and data. For now it's for markets to debate which side is right.

headlines.headline: Nobody wants a data center, dude. And the people that want them seem kind of evil.
headlines.attribution: Theo Von, This Past Weekend podcast
headlines.summary: Theo Von's podcast rant — warning of an emotional credit score and AI becoming a new god — packs a remarkable number of misperceptions into one paragraph. NLW argues Von reflects an emerging mainstream view rather than leading it.

headlines.headline: Data center opposition has gone fully bipartisan
headlines.summary: Erin Brockovich launched a campaign against data centers, former Tea Party conservatives are planning nationwide protests, and comedian Charlie Berens called opposition 'the most bipartisan issue since beer.' The two recurring complaints: water use and electricity prices.

headlines.headline: Amazon's entire data center water use is a day of golf-course watering
headlines.summary: Amazon's global data centers used 2.5 billion gallons in 2025 (down 2% even as footprint grew). US golf courses use over 500 billion gallons a year — meaning Amazon's full year is roughly one day of US golf maintenance. California almonds use 1.2–1.8 trillion gallons, 5–8x all US data centers.

headlines.headline: Leaky US pipes waste 15x what data centers use
headlines.summary: One study found the US loses 3.29 trillion gallons a year to leaky pipes — about 15 times total data center water use. In Indiana, the contested Amazon facility's 300 million gallons a year equals roughly 0.2% of the state's domestic water use.

headlines.headline: A foundational water myth was off by 1,000x
headlines.summary: Author Karen Hao's claim in Empire of AI that a Chile Google data center consumed 1,000x the surrounding population's water was wrong by a factor of 1,000 due to a unit mix-up. She acknowledged and corrected the error — but the book is still out there making the claim.

headlines.headline: If you don't like what the water's used for, even one gallon is too many
headlines.summary: NLW's core diagnosis of the water debate: big numbers are politically potent because most people have no sense of how much water we actually consume. The astronomical-sounding figures grab attention regardless of context.

headlines.headline: No significant correlation between data centers and a state's power prices
headlines.summary: The Institute for Energy Research found prices in the top-ten data center states are virtually identical to the average elsewhere, with no significant relationship to faster rate increases. Most price pressure traces to upgrading an aging grid — a cost we'd face anyway.

headlines.headline: The local short-run story is real — and it's a grid problem
headlines.summary: A Bloomberg analysis of 25,000 grid nodes found wholesale prices rose as much as 276% since 2020 near data center clusters, with 70%+ of increases within 50 miles of significant activity. The Daily Economy's read: not evidence of incompatibility, but that grid infrastructure and cost-allocation rules haven't kept up.

headlines.headline: Policy is racing to make data centers pay their own way
headlines.summary: Oregon's POWER Act requires the biggest users to bear the cost of infrastructure built specifically for them. The White House's Ratepayer Protection Pledge has companies committing to build/buy new power supply, pay for delivery upgrades, pay whether they use the power or not, and invest in local jobs and resilience.

headlines.headline: Labor unions may be the key actor for finding the middle
headlines.summary: NLW argues unions sit in both camps — sympathetic to community concerns that AI disproportionately benefits the rich, yet seeing firsthand how valuable the build-out is as demand for skilled blue-collar work climbs. They're positioned to broker the practical middle ground.

headlines.headline: Two things can be true: data centers have drawbacks, but communities can win far more than they realize.
headlines.summary: The Information's reporting from rural data center projects found communities can extract far more financial concessions than they think — they're being taught the choice is roll over or grab pitchforks, while missing a massive middle path.

headlines.headline: A Louisiana parish gave teachers $50,000 bonuses from a Meta campus
headlines.summary: In rural Richland Parish, hundreds of teachers are set to receive unprecedented $50,000 bonuses funded by a surge in tax receipts tied to Meta's AI campus. An existing ordinance gives teachers a slice of sales taxes; bonuses quintupled from construction activity — a concrete example of the 'how everyone wins' phase NLW wants.
